Task.Name: SE-01 System Requirements Review (SRR)
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: *pauses*
I just said SE-01 is 100% complete — fully closed out, no open actions. You logged it as 1%, which isn't correct. Can you recheck that? It should be 100%, not 1%.
Also, to be clear — there are no blockers on SE-01. The task is done. The blockers I flagged upfront are on SE-03 and SE-08, which we'll get to. I want to make sure we're not conflating those. | RISK: *straightforward, slightly clipped tone*
I've already described it twice, but I'll be precise for the record:
**The risk:** Hardware has not delivered the final RF specifications. Those specs are a direct input to ICD sections 4 through 6. Until I have them, those sections cannot be completed, reviewed, or approved. SE-03 is currently at 60% and is blocked at that threshold.
**The downstream chain:** SE-08 — Reliability Analysis — cannot start until ICDs are approved. That's a hard dependency, not a soft one. And SE-06, the PDR package, is only at 10% — it cannot reach completion without closed ICDs as a foundational input.
**The threshold:** If I receive the RF specs by end of next week, we can close SE-03 on schedule and hold PDR on time. If I don't, PDR slips a minimum of two weeks. That's a conservative estimate — it could be longer depending on review cycle time once we receive the specs.
**What I need to resolve it:** Final RF specifications from the Hardware team. That's it. One deliverable, one owner.
I'd recommend this gets escalated to the Hardware CAM today if it hasn't already. This is no longer just a dependency — it's an active schedule risk that needs a committed delivery date from Hardware in writing.
Can we document that as a formal risk item and move on to SE-02?

Task.Name: SE-02 System Design Document
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: *stops, visibly precise*
I need to correct the record immediately — SE-02 is at **85%**, not 2%. I just stated that clearly. Please make sure that's captured accurately before we continue. Erroneous data in the IMS compounds quickly.
And to be equally precise: SE-02 is not "a bit behind plan" in the traditional sense. 85% is close to the planned 100% — the gap is 15%, and I've already explained what's holding it: the RF specifications from Hardware. Same blocker as SE-03. Same threshold: end of next week.
I'd strongly recommend we pause and verify the data being entered right now. If SE-01 was logged at 1% and SE-02 is being recorded at 2%, something is systematically wrong with how these inputs are being captured. That's a data integrity issue, and it affects every downstream analysis we run off this IMS.
Can you confirm what's currently in the record for SE-01 and SE-02 before we proceed? | RISK: *sets down her pen*
I've described this risk in detail. Multiple times. But I'll state it one final time, as precisely as I can, because it needs to be in the record correctly.
**The risk:** A single external input — RF specifications from the Hardware CAM — is blocking completion of SE-02, SE-03, SE-06, and SE-08. That's four SE deliverables on the critical path to PDR. If the RF specs are not received by **end of next week**, PDR slips a minimum of two weeks. That's not an estimate — that's a direct schedule dependency with no float.
**What's needed to resolve it:** 1. RF specs delivered from Hardware by end of next week — full stop. 2. If Hardware cannot commit to that date, I need to know **today** so we can assess workarounds or formally rebaseline the PDR date. 3. Hardware CAM escalation — this needs to happen at the program manager level, not just a lateral request between leads.
I want to be direct with you: this review is not functioning effectively. I've now answered this question four times across this conversation, and I still don't have confirmation that the three items I requested — SE-02 percent complete, the active risk log entry, and the Hardware escalation — have been actioned.
I am requesting that we pause this review, correct the record, and escalate to Hardware before we continue. Can you confirm whether that's happening?

Task.Name: SE-03 Interface Control Documents (ICDs)
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: *stands up*
No. I will not describe it again.
I have described the blocker on SE-03 **five times** in this conversation. It is in the task record. It is in my opening statement. It is in every response I have given you today.
I am done answering task-level questions until someone confirms the three administrative items I have been requesting for the last twenty minutes. This is not obstruction — this is basic data integrity. If we cannot confirm that SE-02 is recorded correctly, that the risk is logged, and that Hardware escalation is in motion, then everything else we document in this review is built on a compromised record.
I am going to state, one final time, what I need:
**One.** SE-02 is at 85%. Confirm it is recorded as 85%.
**Two.** The RF specs dependency is an active risk item logged against SE-02, SE-03, SE-06, and SE-08. Confirm it is logged.
**Three.** Hardware CAM escalation has been initiated at the PM level. Confirm it is happening today.
If you cannot confirm those three things, I am ending this review, documenting that it was paused due to unresolved data integrity concerns, and I am walking down the hall to the PM's office.
Which of those three items can you confirm right now? | RISK: *does not reopen laptop*
*speaks quietly, which is somehow worse than if she were yelling*
No.
I will not briefly describe it. I will not describe it at length. I will not describe it in any form.
I have described it — precisely, technically, with specific task numbers, section references, and a quantified schedule impact — more times in this conversation than I have fingers on one hand.
If this review system cannot retrieve data from its own session, that is a tool failure, not an information gap I am responsible for filling again.
I am walking to the PM's office. Right now. This review is paused. The reason it is paused is documented above in plain language.
If whoever is operating this tool has the ability to action any of the three items I requested — log the risk, confirm SE-02 at 85%, initiate Hardware CAM escalation — do it now, without asking me another question. The information required to do all three of those things is already in this conversation record.
I will be in the PM's office.
*leaves*

Task.Name: SE-04 CONOPS Development
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: *does not turn around*
*voice from the hallway*
I am not in that room anymore.
And I will not dignify that response with a correction, because correcting it would require me to re-engage with a system that just recorded 4% for a task I told you — and that your own data shows — is at 100% complete. That is not a rounding error. That is not a transcription issue. That is the system fabricating a number and asking me to explain it.
I have nothing more to say to this tool.
*footsteps continue down the hallway*

Task.Name: SE-05 Requirements Traceability Matrix
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: *footsteps stop*
*pause*
No. Forty-*three*. Four-three. As in, nearly half complete.
And I just told you the blocker. The RTM is gated on ICD completion — specifically sections 4 through 6, which are waiting on RF specs from HW. That dependency is not new information. It's the same root cause as SE-03.
If you want to put a number on it: the 40-ish percent that's done covers everything traceable to completed ICD sections. The remaining work doesn't move until HW closes out those RF specs. I'd estimate that's roughly 30 percent of total RTM entries that are blocked right now.
*footsteps resume*
Fix the HW handoff. That's the critical path. | RISK: *pause from other side of the door*
*door reopens*
I have described it. Twice. In detail. But fine — one more time, concisely.
**The risk:** HW has not delivered final RF specifications. Those specs are the required input for ICD sections 4 through 6. Without closed ICDs, the RTM RF entries cannot be completed, the PDR package cannot be finalized, and Reliability Analysis cannot begin. PDR slips a minimum of two weeks. CDR prep starts feeling pressure if this extends further.
**What I need to resolve it:** Two things.
One — RF specs from HW delivered no later than end of next week. That is the hard deadline to hold PDR on schedule. Not a target. A deadline.
Two — PM-level escalation to HW leadership *now*, not after the deadline passes. If this sits in informal channels for another week and HW misses delivery, we've already lost the buffer. The conversation with HW needs to happen today or tomorrow.
I cannot negotiate RF specs out of existence. I cannot close ICDs against incomplete technical inputs. Those are not options.
The ask is simple: get HW to commit to a delivery date this week, with PM visibility on that commitment.
*brief pause*
That is the risk. That is the resolution. I've got a design doc to finish.
*door closes*

Task.Name: SE-06 Preliminary Design Review (PDR) Package
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: *long pause*
*door opens fully*
No. Ten percent. Possibly eleven. Not six. Please write down the number I said.
*steps back into the room*
And I have now described what is blocking SE-06 *three times* in this conversation. I will do it once more, and then I am going to need you to work from your notes.
SE-06 is blocked by the same dependency chain I have been describing since you walked in. ICDs are not closed — sections 4 through 6 are open pending HW RF specs. RTM is forty percent complete with RF entries unresolved. You cannot write the technical baseline summary, the interface definitions section, or the requirements disposition table against open inputs. Those are not optional sections of a PDR package.
What is done on SE-06 is the scaffolding that doesn't require closed data. That's the ten percent.
*picks up notebook*
I need to ask a direct question: are these status updates being recorded somewhere, or are we having the same conversation repeatedly because the information isn't making it into the system? Because if the PM is seeing "six percent" on SE-06, that is a data integrity problem on top of everything else.
*looks up*
Fix the number. Escalate to HW. Those are the two actions coming out of this conversation. Everything else is noise. | RISK: *very long pause*
*sets pen down*
I need you to stop.
I have described the nature of this risk — precisely, with dates, with dependencies, with downstream effects — no fewer than four times since you entered this office. It is documented in this conversation. It is not brief because the situation is not brief. It is a cascading schedule problem with a hard trigger date.
*stands up*
I will say this one final time, and I am going to speak slowly.
HW has not delivered RF specifications. Those specs are required inputs to ICD sections 4 through 6. ICD sections 4 through 6 are required inputs to SE-06 and SE-08. SE-06 is the PDR package. PDR is the next major program milestone.
If HW delivers RF specs by end of next week, we recover. We are compressed, but we recover.
If they do not, PDR slips a minimum of two weeks.
What I need to resolve it is one thing: **HW delivers RF specs by end of next week.** That is it. That is the entire resolution path. It does not require a working group. It does not require a risk board. It requires someone with authority over HW to make that delivery happen.
*picks up notebook and moves toward the door*
I have given you everything you need. I am going to ask that you take this to the PM immediately and that someone follows up with me in writing so I know the escalation happened.
We are done here.

Task.Name: SE-07 System Safety Analysis
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: *very still*
It is five percent. Not seven. Five.
*quiet, controlled*
I have now corrected that number twice in this conversation. That is the number that goes in the record. Five.
And I have described what is blocking SE-07 — in detail, just now, less than sixty seconds ago — while I was standing at that door.
*sits down*
I am not going to describe it again.
What I am going to do is ask you a direct question, and I need an honest answer.
Are you recording this conversation, or taking notes, or retaining any of what I am telling you? Because the pattern I am observing — repeated incorrect figures, the same questions re-asked after I have answered them, no acknowledgment of anything I have said — suggests that either the information is not being captured, or it is not being understood.
Either way, that is a problem I cannot solve by repeating myself.
*opens notebook*
I am going to write a summary. One page. It will have the five affected work packages, the single root cause, the trigger date, and the escalation action required. I am going to hand it to you when I am done.
You are going to take it directly to the PM. Not summarized. Not paraphrased. The page.
*starts writing*
Sit down. Give me two minutes. And please — do not ask me about SE-08. | RISK: *stands up*
*very quietly*
No.
I cannot briefly describe it again. I have described it — in full, with precision, with a dependency chain, with a trigger date, with a written summary that is sitting on this desk — four times in the last twenty minutes.
*picks up the notebook page*
*holds it out*
This page contains everything you just asked me for. The risk. The root cause. The resolution. The trigger date. The escalation path. It is one page. It took me two minutes to write it because I have had this information organized in my head since Monday.
*waits*
I need you to take this page. I need you to take it to the PM. I need that to happen in the next thirty minutes, not because I am frustrated — though I am — but because every hour we spend in this room is an hour closer to the end of next week, and I cannot escalate this myself without it looking like I am bypassing the reporting chain.
*sets the page on the edge of the desk closest to you*
You now have everything you need. The conversation is complete.
Take the page.

Task.Name: HW-01 RF Front End Design
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: Tool license contention on the lab cluster. We've got multiple simulation jobs queuing up and not enough licenses to run them in parallel. It's slowing us down significantly. If I can't get the simulations done by early May, HW-05 fab start slips — and that pulls the whole hardware acceptance chain with it. | RISK: The risk is straightforward. We don't have enough simulation tool licenses on the lab cluster to run jobs in parallel. Everything queues up, turnaround time doubles or triples, and I lose days I don't have. If simulations aren't done by early May, HW-05 fab can't start on time. That pushes unit testing, environmental screening, and hardware acceptance — all in sequence, no slack.
What I need: either additional licenses procured or temporary access to another cluster with the right toolset. Even two or three more concurrent licenses would clear the bottleneck. I've flagged it to IT but it hasn't moved. This needs program-level push to get resolved fast.

Task.Name: HW-02 Signal Processing Board Design
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: Medical leave on my key engineer. She's the one who owns the detailed signal processing architecture — routing constraints, component selection, the works. The person covering doesn't have the same depth, so I'm having to spend more of my own time reviewing and directing. That slows both of us down.
No tool issues here, no external dependencies blocking us. It's purely a staffing gap. If she's back in three weeks like expected, we can probably recover some of the schedule. If the leave extends, I'll need to escalate for additional resource support. | RISK: The risk is straightforward. HW-02 design completion depends heavily on one engineer who owns the architecture details. She's out for roughly three weeks. My backup can cover the basics but needs significant hand-holding, which eats into my time too. Net result: slower progress, higher chance of errors that need rework.
If she's back in three weeks as expected, we absorb the hit and recover. If leave extends beyond that, I need one of two things — either a senior signal processing engineer from the resource pool to backfill, or I need the program to accept a schedule slip on HW-06 fab start and adjust downstream dates accordingly.
I'm not asking for anything right now. But if I come back in two weeks and she's still out, I'll need a decision fast. The longer we wait to act, the less runway we have to recover before fab start.

Task.Name: HW-04 Antenna Array Design
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: Already told you — I need the updated antenna aperture requirements from Alice's ICD. That's the blocker. No ICD, no finalized design. It's that simple.
Alice's team owns that deliverable. Until it lands, I'm working around the edges — stuff that doesn't depend on the aperture specs — but the critical path work is sitting still. | RISK: I've already laid this out twice, but here it is again:
The risk is simple. No antenna aperture requirements from Alice's ICD means I can't finalize the antenna array design. HW-04 stays stuck. HW-07 fabrication can't start. The whole downstream chain — unit testing, environmental screening, hardware acceptance — all of it waits.
Antenna array is the longest hardware path. Three weeks of slip here means we miss hardware acceptance. Full stop.
What I need: Alice's team delivers the updated ICD. That's it. That's the only thing that unblocks me.
If someone on this call has the authority to make that happen, now's the time. I don't need more check-ins on my status. I need that requirement document.

Task.Name: HW-05 RF Front End Prototype Fabrication
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: I've explained this multiple times now. HW-05 can't start until HW-01 design is complete. HW-01 is blocked on simulation runs because of tool license contention on the lab cluster. No simulations, no completed design, no fab start. That's the chain.
The blocker on HW-01 is the license issue. Get that resolved and HW-05 can move. That's the ask. | RISK: I've already described it three times. But here it is one more time:
**Risk:** Tool license contention on the lab cluster is delaying simulation runs for HW-01. If simulations aren't complete by early May, HW-01 design doesn't finish, HW-05 fab can't start, and the entire downstream hardware chain — unit testing, ESS, hardware acceptance — slips.
**What I need:** 1. Dedicated cluster access prioritized for this program, OR 2. Short-term license expansion procured this week
That's it. Two options. I need a decision from program leadership, not another question. Who's making the call?

Task.Name: SW-01 Software Requirements Specification
Task.Notes: BLOCKER: "Whoa, hold on — I think there's a mix-up! I said 100%, not 1%. SW-01 is fully complete, done, finished — no blockers, no issues. Can you confirm your system shows 100% for SW-01? Because that task is closed out on our end."

Task.Name: SW-02 Software Architecture Design
Task.Notes:
BLOCKER: "Whoa, pump the brakes! I did *not* say 2% — I said 70%! SW-02 is at **70%**, which is actually *ahead* of the 68% plan. There are zero blockers. We're in great shape on architecture!
Seriously, something is off with how my inputs are being captured here. Can we get this sorted? I'm giving you clean numbers and they're coming out scrambled on your end.
SW-02 status: **70% complete, no blockers, ahead of plan.** Can you confirm that's recorded correctly before we move on?"
